item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview log is very effective and conveying a keen and sharp sense of growing dread. I was on the edge of my seat while reading this, despite the action being almost nothing at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Purely coincidental. I hadn't read this until you linked it here.

Having just read it, I'd argue coin tosses represent different things in these two stories. In the play, the coin signals that the two characters are in an "un-, sub-, or supernatural" dimension, i.e. Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are dead; and the reactions of the characters present dramatic irony. In this piece, the coin conveys the fragility of human existence, i.e. we're just one coin toss away from wiping out as a species; and the reaction of the SCP illustrates a persistent frustration that the probabilities don't add up and that humans just won't die.

But I do agree they serve similar purposes: Showing that something spooky is afoot. Here, I'd argue that, historically, people have flipped coins and other things long enough that it wouldn't be unusual if a famous tragicomedy and a lowly SCP article shared it as a plot device.

That said, I appreciate the opportunity to read Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are Dead. So thank you for posting!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Novote for now. Will change to an upvote if it turns out this was coincidental and not just plagiarized from Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are Dead

"We have been spinning coins together since—This is not the first time we spun coins!!"
"Oh no - we've been spinning coins for as long as I remember."
"How long is that?"
"I forget."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. Especially since the first time seemed to imply someone… other was also present, and the dialogue in that instant seemed like it could have been from someone else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this overall! The only thing I'd note is that some of the spacing within individual characters' speech patterns made the pacing odd at times. For example:

Dr. Testa: Okay. Another question, then.

You’re saying you came here to see the face. Which implies that you haven’t flipped heads before.

With how full spaces typically are used to indicate a change in speaking character, it takes a second for me to register that this is the same character in both lines. I'm not sure if you're using this intentionally to some specific purpose, but it did make some of the dialogue feel awkward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This starts off small, but it has surprising power. The narrative coin stays nicely balanced on the edge. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I totally forgot about this, nice to see it posted!

item.title: Coin Toss
item.content:encoded:
A man, a woman, and a coin walk into a cell.

My submission for the Cliché-Con 2019 Contest. This story implements a Category 2 cliché:
An interview in which the anomaly is overly cryptic, followed by the interviewer suffering.

I have no cookie, and I must thank.
I have no cookie, and I must thank:

Modern_Erasmus and FortuneFavorsBold for brainstorming help.
Aismallard, A_Sack_Of_Potatoes, BoogeyTheMan23, JackIke, Laonasa, and Zyn for draft critique.

Special thanks to ShaggyDredlocks for their valuable insight on how to simplify this story into one about fate.

If I forget anyone or use the wrong username, please let me know.
